sections.instruction_text:
Part 1 (15 marks)
We start our analysis of the VAT on private schools with a broad overview of the costs
and benefits ofthe policy using concepts wehavelearned in the course. Such asummary
can easily be generated from a generative AI software. However, itis important to cross-
check that the AI is not hallucinating, which is a common issue, and to edit the AI’s
output so that it has your own writing tone and emphasises the points that you, as an
economist, think are important.
1. Using a publicly available generative AI platform of your choice (e.g. chatGPT,
Claude, Gemini), generate a 300 word summary of the VAT policy. The summary
should cover the main changes introduced by the policy and its likely costs and
benefits. Report both the prompt that you used and the output that you received
from the platform.
2. Using the course material and the IFS report provided, comment on whether the
outputgeneratedseemsaccurate. Youshouldgooverthemainclaimsintheoutput
andfindspecificquotesfromthereportorthecoursematerialthatsupport,qualify,
or contradict the claims. Indicate the relevant page numbers (from the article or
the lecture slides).
3. Edit the output to correct any mistakes or inaccuracies that you found, support it

subparts.label: Part 2 Q6
subparts.instruction_text:
6. [10 marks] Discuss carefully, in your own words, whether you think the introduc-
tion of VAT on private schools is a good idea. Use the results from the analysis of
the theoretical model to illustrate your argument and discuss the intuition behind
these results. Pay particular attention to discussing which groups would benefit or
lose more from the introduction of the VAT. Make references to concept studied
in the lecture wherever relevant. [Suggested word count: 300 words]

subparts.label: Part 3 Q1
subparts.instruction_text:
1. [7.5 marks] Given the results you have derived from the model above, list some
predictions that the theory makes about the relationship between variables in the
model. Note: a prediction is a statement of the kind “when variable X increases,
variable Y decreases”, or “when variable X increases, variable Y decreases”. It can
also take the form of “when variable X increases, variable Y decreases, but only if
variable Z is sufficiently high”

subparts.label: Part 3 Q2
subparts.instruction_text:
2. [7.5 marks] For each prediction from the theory, explain how each variable could
bemeasuredintherealworld. Findsomedatasourcesonlinethatcouldbeusedto
measure these variables. Note: you do not need to download the data, just provide
a link to the source of data and indicate which variable or combination of variables
in the dataset you would use.

subparts.label: Part 3 Q3
subparts.instruction_text:
3. [7.5 marks] Choose one of the theory’s prediction from your answer to question
1 above, and write down the regression equation that would allow you to test
the prediction using the data you identified in question 2. Explain carefully each
term in your equation, identify the main coefficient of interest and describe what
hypothesis you would test to check if the theory is correct.

subparts.label: Part 3 Q4
subparts.instruction_text:
4. [7.5 marks] Explain carefully why the regression might suffer from endogeneity
issues, using the theoretical model from Part 2 whenever relevant, and propose
somesolutionsthatwouldallowyoutoaddresstheseendogeneityconcerns. Discuss
whether these solutions could be implemented in practice.

sections.label: Part 4
sections.instruction_text:
Part 4 (15 marks)
Discuss how the theoretical model from Part 2, and the evidence we could get to
test it (as derived in Part 3), can shed light on the various arguments brought up on
the two Youtube videos posted in the documents (https://www.youtube.com/shorts/
PPEbYZykG1Aandhttps://www.youtube.com/shorts/Gh-YtzX3Hcc). Whatarguments
are brought up in the video but not included in the model? How would you change the
model to analyse these points? [Suggested word count: 300 words]

Part 1 (15 marks)
We start our analysis of the VAT on private schools with a broad overview of the costs
and benefits ofthe policy using concepts wehavelearned in the course. Such asummary
can easily be generated from a generative AI software. However, itis important to cross-
check that the AI is not hallucinating, which is a common issue, and to edit the AI’s
output so that it has your own writing tone and emphasises the points that you, as an
economist, think are important.
1. Using a publicly available generative AI platform of your choice (e.g. chatGPT,
Claude, Gemini), generate a 300 word summary of the VAT policy. The summary
should cover the main changes introduced by the policy and its likely costs and
benefits. Report both the prompt that you used and the output that you received
from the platform.
2. Using the course material and the IFS report provided, comment on whether the
outputgeneratedseemsaccurate. Youshouldgooverthemainclaimsintheoutput
andfindspecificquotesfromthereportorthecoursematerialthatsupport,qualify,
or contradict the claims. Indicate the relevant page numbers (from the article or
the lecture slides).
3. Edit the output to correct any mistakes or inaccuracies that you found, support it
with references from the article or lecture notes, and rewrite it in your own tone.
Note: your answer should contain four parts: (1) the prompt you submitted, (2) the
output from the generative AI platform, (3) your comments on the output, (4) the re-
written summary.
Answer:
Students should provide a prompt which correctly asks the AI to provide a 300-word
summary of the introduction of VAT on private school fees and its likely positive and
negative effects. Prompts that do not specifically refer to the VAT on private school fees
or that ask the AI to summarise the IFS report do not address the task correctly.
In the comments on the AI output, students are expected to ensure that some key features
of the policy are accurately covered, including:
• How it changes the existing policies.
1
• Do the likely positive effects mentioned by the AI include higher fiscal revenue and
higher social mobility? Are these positive effects framed in terms of the positive
externalities of education studied in Lecture 9, the equity argument discussed in
lecture 1 and the role of redistribution discussed in lecture 2. Is the forecasted
magnitude of the positive effects in line with the figures mentioned in the IFS
report?
• Do the likely negative effects mentioned by the AI include large class sizes in public
schools? Are these negative effects framed in terms of the tax distortions discussed
in lecture 3? Is the forecasted magnitude of the negative effects in line with the
figures mentioned in the IFS report?
Marks should be allocated based on the student’s ability to (1) identify what is a contro-
versial claim in the generated output, (2) what evidence provided in the report addresses
this claim, (3) which concepts in the lecture notes are relevant to evaluate these claims.
References to the lecture slides and the report should be clearly identified with quotes, and
page or slide numbers and the arguments provided in these sources should be carefully
explained.

3. To evaluate the impact of the VAT increase on proportion of school children at-
tending public school, we could run the following regression:
y = α+β ×VAT +β X +ε
t 1 t 2 t t
Where
• The unit of observation t is the year of the observation.
• The dependent variable y would be the share of pupils in England attending
t
public school vs. those attending private schools.
• The independent variable VAT is a dummy variable taking value 1 in the first
t
year after the VAT increase, i.e., 2025.
14
• X is a set of control variables that could include for example some proxy for
t
other factors affecting the decision of going to private school such as aver-
age income, relative school quality in the previous year (e.g. as measured by
success on GCSEs or A-levels in public vs. private schools), etc.
The coefficient of interest is β . The theory predicts that the introduction of VAT
1
will lead to an increase in public school attendance, so our null hypothesis should
be H : β = 0 and alternative hypothesis is H : β > 0. A t-test of this hypothesis
0 1 1 1
can tell us whether the relationship is statistically significant.
4. Endogeneity issues arise when the independent variable is systematically correlated
with the error term. This can arise for two reasons: unobserved omitted variables
which are systematically correlated with both the introduction of VAT and public
school attendance and reverse causality, whereby the introduction of VAT is deter-
mined by the level of public school attendance.
The main concerns with this regression is due to unobserved omitted variables.
Besides the control variables listed above, there could be other factors that can affect
the decision to send children to private school that change over time but cannot be
measured. For instance, parents’ taste for private school might be changing year-
on-year (captured by parameters P , P and P in the model) and be correlated
M H L
with other political preferences (e.g. preferences for redistribution). This only
createsanendogeneityissueifchangesinthesefactorsaresystematicallycorrelated
with the introduction of VAT.
For instance, this could the case here because an increase in preferences for re-
distribution across the population might have been what led to the election of the
Labour party, who introduced the VAT. If people’s preferences for redistributions
are correlated with their preferences for public school over private school (i.e., a
lower P ), then an increase in preferences for redistribution would both lead to the
i
VAT being more likely to be introduced AND an increase in attendance in public
schools, EVEN IF there were no direct causal effect from the VAT increase to the
attendance decrease.
This does not mean the increase in VAT does not lead to an increase in public
school attendance, but that the effect of the VAT could be overestimated, as some
of the effect could be simply driven by the confounding variables. If changes in
political preferences towards Labour are positively correlated with a change in pref-
erences towards public school, then our estimate of β would overestimate the true
1
treatment effect. Without a measure of the underlying change in preferences, we
cannotidentify howmuch ofthe changeinthe proportion ofchildren goingto public
school is due to the VAT or a change in taste.
Some possible solutions would be to use time trends from previous years to net
out the change over time, if there is a clear linear trend. To do this, we could
for example run a difference-in-differences specification across months and local
authorities where the treatment group are the months around the introduction of
15
the reform but in the year prior to the reform. This would address the endogeneity
issue by subtracting the underlying long-term change in unobserved preferences for
public school from the change in public school attendance around the time of the
VAT introduction.
Alternatively, we could conduct a difference-in-differences by comparing the share
of public school attendance in Northern Ireland to the share in counties in the Re-
public of Ireland close to the border, since the Republic of Ireland did not introduce
a VAT on private schools. In this case, the treatment group would be counties in
Northern Ireland and the control group would be counties in the Republic of Ire-
land. This would address the endogeneity issue because omitted variables such as
preferences for public school are unlikely to change systematically differently across
each side of the border so subtracting the difference over time in attendance in the
Republic of Ireland would net out the effect of change in unobservable preferences.

Part 4 (15 marks)
Discuss how the theoretical model from Part 2, and the evidence we could get to
test it (as derived in Part 3), can shed light on the various arguments brought up on
the two Youtube videos posted in the documents (https://www.youtube.com/shorts/
PPEbYZykG1Aandhttps://www.youtube.com/shorts/Gh-YtzX3Hcc). Whatarguments
are brought up in the video but not included in the model? How would you change the
model to analyse these points? [Suggested word count: 300 words]
Answer: Students should make specific references to the claims made in each video and
for each claim indicate clearly which element of the model helps support or contradict
the claim.
For example, in the first video (The danger of VAT on school fees), the following argu-
ments are made:
• The VAT on private school fees will increase cost of private school and mean fami-
lies who send their children there will have less disposable income to spend on other
things (at 18 seconds into the video). This is a point covered in the IFS report but
not covered in the model.
Introducing it in the model is unlikely to change the main conclusion about the
changes in attendance but could change the consequences about social welfare if the
lower spending power would have had a larger multiplier on GDP than the fiscal
multiplier from redistributing towards public education (captured by the coefficient
of 5 on T in the model).
To introduce it in the model, we would need to model the consumption and saving
decisions of families, in addition to their decision over where to send their children.
For instance, we could say that the utility of a family from sending their child to
16
√
private school is equal to V (c,s,x) = u (c)+P + (F) subject to c+(1+τ)t+S ≤ I
i i i y
where I is the family’s income and c is their consumption. As a result, an increase
in VAT would make the budget constraint more binding and force the family to
reduce c. To measure the effect on social welfare, we would need to introduce some
parameterintheutilityofallfamiliesthatdependsontheaggregateconsumptionor
alternatively have a full general equilibrium model of production and consumption.
The importance of the multiplier in determining the effect of the policy on welfare
is missing from the video but highlighted in the lecture when discussing the positive
externalities of education (Lecture 9) and the negative externalities of inequalities
(Lecture 1). The model captures it through the coefficient of 5 on the term T in
the utility of public school.
• If the VAT does not lead to reduced consumption, it will lead to people pulling
their children out of private schools (at 26 seconds into the video). This argument
is captured by the model as we showed in Part 2 question 4 that an increase in
VAT can lead to an increase in public school attendance. In particular, the term
S captures the potential effect of overcrowding that arises when x increases as a
x
result of the VAT.
However, the video misses some important point we learnt from the model. First,
the decrease in public school capacity could be outweighed by the increase in funding
(T). In fact, this is the main point made by the government and highlighted in
the IFS report (as we learned in Part 1). Second, the decrease in private school
attendance could affect the amount of VAT raised directly. The first point qualifies
the fear that the VAT might be detrimental to public schools, as we have shown in
part 2 question 5 that the net effect on social welfare could still be positive. The
second point would reinforce the argument from the presenter but is not mentioned.
• People who would have sent their children to private school might now decide to
send them to public school, further decreasing the capacity in public school. This is
essentially the same argument as the previous one and is therefore also captured,
but qualified, by the model.
• The increase in public school attendance will lead teachers striking. This point is
not captured directly by the model, though we could argue that this is one of the
negative effects captured by the S term. What the model shows is that as long as
x
the increase in funding T outweighs the increase in attendance x, then the policy
has a net positive effect on social welfare. That’s the case regardless of whether
the effect of increased attendance goes through larger class sizes or strike from
teachers. If we wanted to introduce it in the model, we could add an additional
agent which represents teachers and allow them to choose between striking or not
striking, where striking comes at a cost but can increase their payoff (e.g. obtain a
higher wage). The decision to strike could be influenced by the number of children
in public school, x, thus capturing the argument of the presenter, and strike would
in turn be introduced as a negative effect on the public school children’s utility,
17
whenever teachers choose to strike. However, ultimately, the effect of overcrowding
on strikes and in turn the effect of strikes on the utility from public school would be
very similar to the effect captured by the term S so adding this to the model would
x
not add much.
Similar discussions could be made about the arguments made in the second video.
Note: thisisanexampleofargumentsthatcouldbemade. Thewordlimitwillnecessarily
constrain how much the student can discuss, but the key elements for a high grade would
be for the student to discuss specific arguments made in the videos, clearly connect them
to the model, and suggest specific changes to address the limitations of the model.

sections.weight_text: Public schools are free but private schools have tuition fees equal to t pounds a year. In addition to the tuition fees, families attending private school may need to pay VAT on these fees. Let τ be the VAT rate. There are three families who can attend these schools: a low-income family (L), a middle-income family (M), and a high-income family (H). Each family has one child.

Part 1 (15 marks)
We start our analysis of the VAT on private schools with a broad overview of the costs
and benefits ofthe policy using concepts wehavelearned in the course. Such asummary
can easily be generated from a generative AI software. However, itis important to cross-
check that the AI is not hallucinating, which is a common issue, and to edit the AI’s
output so that it has your own writing tone and emphasises the points that you, as an
economist, think are important.
1. Using a publicly available generative AI platform of your choice (e.g. chatGPT,
Claude, Gemini), generate a 300 word summary of the VAT policy. The summary
should cover the main changes introduced by the policy and its likely costs and
benefits. Report both the prompt that you used and the output that you received
from the platform.
2. Using the course material and the IFS report provided, comment on whether the
outputgeneratedseemsaccurate. Youshouldgooverthemainclaimsintheoutput
andfindspecificquotesfromthereportorthecoursematerialthatsupport,qualify,
or contradict the claims. Indicate the relevant page numbers (from the article or
the lecture slides).
3. Edit the output to correct any mistakes or inaccuracies that you found, support it
with references from the article or lecture notes, and rewrite it in your own tone.
Note: your answer should contain four parts: (1) the prompt you submitted, (2) the
output from the generative AI platform, (3) your comments on the output, (4) the re-
written summary.
Answer:
Students should provide a prompt which correctly asks the AI to provide a 300-word
summary of the introduction of VAT on private school fees and its likely positive and
negative effects. Prompts that do not specifically refer to the VAT on private school fees
or that ask the AI to summarise the IFS report do not address the task correctly.
In the comments on the AI output, students are expected to ensure that some key features
of the policy are accurately covered, including:
• How it changes the existing policies.
1
• Do the likely positive effects mentioned by the AI include higher fiscal revenue and
higher social mobility? Are these positive effects framed in terms of the positive
externalities of education studied in Lecture 9, the equity argument discussed in
lecture 1 and the role of redistribution discussed in lecture 2. Is the forecasted
magnitude of the positive effects in line with the figures mentioned in the IFS
report?
• Do the likely negative effects mentioned by the AI include large class sizes in public
schools? Are these negative effects framed in terms of the tax distortions discussed
in lecture 3? Is the forecasted magnitude of the negative effects in line with the
figures mentioned in the IFS report?
Marks should be allocated based on the student’s ability to (1) identify what is a contro-
versial claim in the generated output, (2) what evidence provided in the report addresses
this claim, (3) which concepts in the lecture notes are relevant to evaluate these claims.
References to the lecture slides and the report should be clearly identified with quotes, and
page or slide numbers and the arguments provided in these sources should be carefully
explained.

1. [7.5 marks] Given the results you have derived from the model above, list some

3. To evaluate the impact of the VAT increase on proportion of school children at-
tending public school, we could run the following regression:
y = α+β ×VAT +β X +ε
t 1 t 2 t t
Where
• The unit of observation t is the year of the observation.
• The dependent variable y would be the share of pupils in England attending
t
public school vs. those attending private schools.
• The independent variable VAT is a dummy variable taking value 1 in the first
t
year after the VAT increase, i.e., 2025.
14
• X is a set of control variables that could include for example some proxy for
t
other factors affecting the decision of going to private school such as aver-
age income, relative school quality in the previous year (e.g. as measured by
success on GCSEs or A-levels in public vs. private schools), etc.
The coefficient of interest is β . The theory predicts that the introduction of VAT
1
will lead to an increase in public school attendance, so our null hypothesis should
be H : β = 0 and alternative hypothesis is H : β > 0. A t-test of this hypothesis
0 1 1 1
can tell us whether the relationship is statistically significant.
4. Endogeneity issues arise when the independent variable is systematically correlated
with the error term. This can arise for two reasons: unobserved omitted variables
which are systematically correlated with both the introduction of VAT and public
school attendance and reverse causality, whereby the introduction of VAT is deter-
mined by the level of public school attendance.
The main concerns with this regression is due to unobserved omitted variables.
Besides the control variables listed above, there could be other factors that can affect
the decision to send children to private school that change over time but cannot be
measured. For instance, parents’ taste for private school might be changing year-
on-year (captured by parameters P , P and P in the model) and be correlated
M H L
with other political preferences (e.g. preferences for redistribution). This only
createsanendogeneityissueifchangesinthesefactorsaresystematicallycorrelated
with the introduction of VAT.
For instance, this could the case here because an increase in preferences for re-
distribution across the population might have been what led to the election of the
Labour party, who introduced the VAT. If people’s preferences for redistributions
are correlated with their preferences for public school over private school (i.e., a
lower P ), then an increase in preferences for redistribution would both lead to the
i
VAT being more likely to be introduced AND an increase in attendance in public
schools, EVEN IF there were no direct causal effect from the VAT increase to the
attendance decrease.
This does not mean the increase in VAT does not lead to an increase in public
school attendance, but that the effect of the VAT could be overestimated, as some
of the effect could be simply driven by the confounding variables. If changes in
political preferences towards Labour are positively correlated with a change in pref-
erences towards public school, then our estimate of β would overestimate the true
1
treatment effect. Without a measure of the underlying change in preferences, we
cannotidentify howmuch ofthe changeinthe proportion ofchildren goingto public
school is due to the VAT or a change in taste.
Some possible solutions would be to use time trends from previous years to net
out the change over time, if there is a clear linear trend. To do this, we could
for example run a difference-in-differences specification across months and local
authorities where the treatment group are the months around the introduction of
15
the reform but in the year prior to the reform. This would address the endogeneity
issue by subtracting the underlying long-term change in unobserved preferences for
public school from the change in public school attendance around the time of the
VAT introduction.
Alternatively, we could conduct a difference-in-differences by comparing the share
of public school attendance in Northern Ireland to the share in counties in the Re-
public of Ireland close to the border, since the Republic of Ireland did not introduce
a VAT on private schools. In this case, the treatment group would be counties in
Northern Ireland and the control group would be counties in the Republic of Ire-
land. This would address the endogeneity issue because omitted variables such as
preferences for public school are unlikely to change systematically differently across
each side of the border so subtracting the difference over time in attendance in the
Republic of Ireland would net out the effect of change in unobservable preferences.

sections.instruction_text:
Part 4 (15 marks)
Discuss how the theoretical model from Part 2, and the evidence we could get to
test it (as derived in Part 3), can shed light on the various arguments brought up on
the two Youtube videos posted in the documents (https://www.youtube.com/shorts/
PPEbYZykG1Aandhttps://www.youtube.com/shorts/Gh-YtzX3Hcc). Whatarguments
are brought up in the video but not included in the model? How would you change the
model to analyse these points? [Suggested word count: 300 words]
Answer: Students should make specific references to the claims made in each video and
for each claim indicate clearly which element of the model helps support or contradict
the claim.
For example, in the first video (The danger of VAT on school fees), the following argu-
ments are made:
• The VAT on private school fees will increase cost of private school and mean fami-
lies who send their children there will have less disposable income to spend on other
things (at 18 seconds into the video). This is a point covered in the IFS report but
not covered in the model.
Introducing it in the model is unlikely to change the main conclusion about the
changes in attendance but could change the consequences about social welfare if the
lower spending power would have had a larger multiplier on GDP than the fiscal
multiplier from redistributing towards public education (captured by the coefficient
of 5 on T in the model).
To introduce it in the model, we would need to model the consumption and saving
decisions of families, in addition to their decision over where to send their children.
For instance, we could say that the utility of a family from sending their child to
16
√
private school is equal to V (c,s,x) = u (c)+P + (F) subject to c+(1+τ)t+S ≤ I
i i i y
where I is the family’s income and c is their consumption. As a result, an increase
in VAT would make the budget constraint more binding and force the family to
reduce c. To measure the effect on social welfare, we would need to introduce some
parameterintheutilityofallfamiliesthatdependsontheaggregateconsumptionor
alternatively have a full general equilibrium model of production and consumption.
The importance of the multiplier in determining the effect of the policy on welfare
is missing from the video but highlighted in the lecture when discussing the positive
externalities of education (Lecture 9) and the negative externalities of inequalities
(Lecture 1). The model captures it through the coefficient of 5 on the term T in
the utility of public school.
• If the VAT does not lead to reduced consumption, it will lead to people pulling
their children out of private schools (at 26 seconds into the video). This argument
is captured by the model as we showed in Part 2 question 4 that an increase in
VAT can lead to an increase in public school attendance. In particular, the term
S captures the potential effect of overcrowding that arises when x increases as a
x
result of the VAT.
However, the video misses some important point we learnt from the model. First,
the decrease in public school capacity could be outweighed by the increase in funding
(T). In fact, this is the main point made by the government and highlighted in
the IFS report (as we learned in Part 1). Second, the decrease in private school
attendance could affect the amount of VAT raised directly. The first point qualifies
the fear that the VAT might be detrimental to public schools, as we have shown in
part 2 question 5 that the net effect on social welfare could still be positive. The
second point would reinforce the argument from the presenter but is not mentioned.
• People who would have sent their children to private school might now decide to
send them to public school, further decreasing the capacity in public school. This is
essentially the same argument as the previous one and is therefore also captured,
but qualified, by the model.
• The increase in public school attendance will lead teachers striking. This point is
not captured directly by the model, though we could argue that this is one of the
negative effects captured by the S term. What the model shows is that as long as
x
the increase in funding T outweighs the increase in attendance x, then the policy
has a net positive effect on social welfare. That’s the case regardless of whether
the effect of increased attendance goes through larger class sizes or strike from
teachers. If we wanted to introduce it in the model, we could add an additional
agent which represents teachers and allow them to choose between striking or not
striking, where striking comes at a cost but can increase their payoff (e.g. obtain a
higher wage). The decision to strike could be influenced by the number of children
in public school, x, thus capturing the argument of the presenter, and strike would
in turn be introduced as a negative effect on the public school children’s utility,
17
whenever teachers choose to strike. However, ultimately, the effect of overcrowding
on strikes and in turn the effect of strikes on the utility from public school would be
very similar to the effect captured by the term S so adding this to the model would
x
not add much.
Similar discussions could be made about the arguments made in the second video.
Note: thisisanexampleofargumentsthatcouldbemade. Thewordlimitwillnecessarily
constrain how much the student can discuss, but the key elements for a high grade would
be for the student to discuss specific arguments made in the videos, clearly connect them
to the model, and suggest specific changes to address the limitations of the model.
18
